title: The first thing I ever heard about money
subtitle: A journalling set for mapping the inherited money beliefs that arrived before you could question them.
excerpt: A journal prompt set for tracing the money beliefs absorbed in childhood: the first thing you heard, who said it, what it meant, and whether it still runs you. Five prompts. Allow twenty to thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: Drawn from The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025), Week 2 prompts on breaking generational money patterns.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to map where your money beliefs came from, especially after a conversation with family has stirred something.

content.text: A journal set for mapping the money beliefs absorbed before you could examine them. Five prompts. Allow twenty to thirty minutes, a timer if you like, and write without editing.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What does money mean to you right now, in one word?
Write the first word. Then write around it: where did that word come from?
What is the earliest money memory you can find?
Hold the memory without analysing it. Write what you saw, heard, felt.
What was the first thing you ever heard about money? Who said it?
A saying, a worry overheard, a rule stated plainly. Name the person if you can.
What did that message say about money, about people who had it, about people who didn't?
Write the implied belief, not just the words.
Is that belief still running your money today? Where do you see it?
Name one specific decision or pattern it shows up in.
Drawn from The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025), Week 2, which addresses breaking generational money patterns through structured reflection.
